---
title: "Physics"
---

This lesson starts with a finite model: phases on directed graph edges, paths that add those phases, and closed loops whose endpoint gauge shifts cancel.

The ordinary baseline is lattice gauge notation. A link variable sits on an oriented edge, a path accumulates link values, and a Wilson loop records a closed-path quantity. Circle Calculus does not replace that notation. It adds a proof-status-safe interface:

```text
finite edge diagram
  -> path record
  -> holonomy calculation
  -> gauge-transform certificate
  -> theorem and dictionary ids
  -> paper, Python, and Lean sidecars
```

::: {.warning-box}
This page is a finite bookkeeping lesson. Its diagrams and Python fixtures are executable references, not proof artifacts. It is not continuum electromagnetism, quantum field theory, Yang-Mills theory, Berry phase, or a physics prediction.
:::

::: {.learning-unit}

## What You Should Learn

By the end of the page, you should be able to separate an open-path endpoint effect from a closed-loop Wilson certificate in a finite `Z_n` phase model.

:::

## Ordinary Baseline

In standard lattice language, a gauge field assigns data to edges. A vertex gauge choice changes the local representatives. Closed-loop quantities are important because endpoint effects cancel around a loop.

That is already standard mathematics. The Circle Calculus contribution is not the idea of a Wilson loop. The contribution is the source-linked record that keeps the diagram, finite calculation, theorem id, dictionary ids, paper section, Python fixture, and Lean target together.

## Circle Calculus Framing

The finite model reuses earlier vocabulary:

```text
circle phase     -> edge phase modulo n
rotation sum     -> path holonomy
closure          -> closed loop
fiber choice     -> vertex gauge representative
certificate      -> source-linked status record
```

The visible path is the base object. The hidden phase choices live at vertices. For an open path, the source and target shifts remain visible. For a closed path, the endpoint is the same node, so those shifts cancel in the finite fixture.

## Executable Fixture

The Python reference model under `circle_math/physics.py` provides:

```text
GaugeEdge
GaugePath
path_holonomy
concat_paths
reverse_path
gauge_transform_path
transformed_holonomy_endpoint_prediction
square_plaquette_path
wilson_loop_certificate
```

The sidecar tests check a composable path, a reversed path, an open-path gauge transform, a closed Wilson loop, and a square plaquette.

::: {.checkpoint-box}

If a path starts at `s` and ends at `t`, which gauge values survive after every interior vertex cancellation?

:::

## Proof Boundary

The theorem cards below are Lean-proved for the finite `ZMod n` phase and link-path model. The Python graph fixtures remain executable references. Their status comes from generated theorem data.

<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0001"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0002"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0003"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0004"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0005"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0006"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0007"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0008"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0009"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0010"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0011"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0012"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0013"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0014"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0015"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0016"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0017"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0018"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0019"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0020"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0021"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0022"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0023"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0024"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0025"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0026"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0027"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0028"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0029"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0030"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0031"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0032"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0033"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0034"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0035"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0036"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0037"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0038"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0039"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0040"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0041"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="PHYS-T0042"></div>

## Dictionary Trail

<div class="dictionary-box" data-dictionary-id="COMMON-0060"></div>
<div class="dictionary-box" data-dictionary-id="COMMON-0061"></div>
<div class="dictionary-box" data-dictionary-id="COMMON-0062"></div>
<div class="dictionary-box" data-dictionary-id="COMMON-0063"></div>

## Source Trail

<p data-paper-ref="papers/physics/PAPER_PHYS_01_PROOF_CARRYING_LATTICE_GAUGE.md">Physics paper: <a href="https://github.com/corbensorenson/circle-calculus/blob/main/papers/physics/PAPER_PHYS_01_PROOF_CARRYING_LATTICE_GAUGE.md">Proof-Carrying Finite Lattice Gauge Links</a></p>
<p data-paper-ref="papers/phase2/PAPER_P2_03_BUNDLE_CALCULUS.md">Bundle paper: <a href="https://github.com/corbensorenson/circle-calculus/blob/main/papers/phase2/PAPER_P2_03_BUNDLE_CALCULUS.md">Bundle Calculus</a></p>
<p data-paper-ref="papers/S3/PAPER_S3_03_HOPF_COILS.md">Hopf paper: <a href="https://github.com/corbensorenson/circle-calculus/blob/main/papers/S3/PAPER_S3_03_HOPF_COILS.md">Hopf Coils</a></p>
<p data-paper-ref="papers/S3/PAPER_S3_04_SPIN_DOUBLE_COVER_ROADMAP.md">Spin paper: <a href="https://github.com/corbensorenson/circle-calculus/blob/main/papers/S3/PAPER_S3_04_SPIN_DOUBLE_COVER_ROADMAP.md">Spin Double Cover Roadmap</a></p>

::: {.warning-box}
The Python fixture is an executable reference. The current Lean model proves finite phase-list and link-path holonomy facts plus singleton, two-link, singleton-concat, three-link, four-link, empty-identity, concat-identity, concat-associativity, reversal-algebra, boundary-append composability, first source/target projection facts, checked finite path endpoint facts, checked finite path identity/associativity laws, checked finite path holonomy identity/singleton/concat laws, and projection bridges from checked paths back to the link-path carrier. A full finite graph path category remains future graph-path work.
:::

<script type="module" src="../../widgets/shared/widget_base.js"></script>
